questions.question: The divergence of F = x2 i + y2 j + z2 k at the point (1, 2, 3) is
If F = grad(phi) for some scalar phi, then F is called
questions.explanation: If F = grad(phi) (gradient of a scalar), F is conservative (also called irrotational since curl F = curl(grad(phi)) = 0). Solenoidal means divergence-free (div F = 0).

questions.question: By Green's theorem, the line integral ∮_C (y dx - x dy) over the unit circle equals
questions.explanation: P=y, Q=-x. Green's theorem: ∬(dQ/dx - dP/dy)dA = ∬(-1-1)dA = -2 x Area(unit circle) = -2pi.

questions.question: The curl of F = y i + x j + 0 k is
questions.explanation: curl F = (dR/dy - dQ/dz)i + (dP/dz - dR/dx)j + (dQ/dx - dP/dy)k. P=y, Q=x, R=0: = 0i + 0j + (1-1)k = 0. F is irrotational.

questions.question: Gauss's divergence theorem relates

options.A: Volume integral of divergence to surface flux integral
options.B: Line integral to surface integral of curl
options.C: Line integral to area integral in 2D
options.D: Surface integral to another surface integral

questions.explanation: Gauss's theorem: ∭_V (div F)dV = ∬_S F.dS. It converts a volume integral (of divergence) to a closed surface integral (flux). Stokes' converts line to surface; Green's converts line to area.

questions.question: The gradient of f(x, y, z) = x2 + y2 + z2 at point (1, 1, 1) is
questions.explanation: grad f = (df/dx)i + (df/dy)j + (df/dz)k = 2xi + 2yj + 2zk. At (1,1,1): grad f = 2i + 2j + 2k. The gradient points in the direction of steepest ascent of f. Its magnitude |grad f| = sqrt(4+4+4) = 2*sqrt(3) is the maximum rate of change.

questions.question: A vector field F is solenoidal if
questions.explanation: A solenoidal (incompressible) vector field has div F = 0 (zero divergence). This means no net flux sources or sinks — what flows in equals what flows out. Examples: magnetic fields (div B = 0 by Maxwell's equations), incompressible fluid velocity fields. Conservative fields have curl F = 0.

questions.question: The line integral ∫_C F.dr where F = xi + yj along the straight line from (0,0) to (1,1) is
questions.explanation: Parameterize: x=t, y=t, t from 0 to 1. dx=dt, dy=dt. F = ti + tj. F.dr = t(dt) + t(dt) = 2t dt. ∫_0^1 2t dt = [t2]_0^1 = 1. Note: F = xi+yj = grad(x2/2 + y2/2) is conservative, so the integral depends only on endpoints: phi(1,1)-phi(0,0) = (1/2+1/2)-(0) = 1.

questions.question: Stokes' theorem relates a surface integral of the curl to

options.A: A line integral around the boundary curve
options.D: A line integral of the gradient

questions.explanation: Stokes' Theorem: ∬_S (curl F).dS = ∮_C F.dr, where C is the boundary curve of surface S. It converts a surface integral of the curl into a line integral around the boundary. Compare: Gauss's theorem converts volume integral to surface integral; Green's theorem is the 2D version of Stokes'.

questions.question: If F is a conservative vector field, the line integral ∫_C F.dr

options.A: Depends only on the endpoints, not the path
options.D: Equals the surface integral of F

questions.explanation: For a conservative field F = grad(phi): ∫_C F.dr = phi(B) - phi(A), which depends only on endpoints A and B, not on the path C. This is the gradient theorem (fundamental theorem for line integrals). The integral IS zero only for closed paths (A=B). Non-conservative fields give path-dependent integrals.

questions.question: The Laplacian of f = x2 + y2 + z2 is
questions.explanation: Laplacian: del2 f = d2f/dx2 + d2f/dy2 + d2f/dz2. d2(x2)/dx2 = 2, d2(y2)/dy2 = 2, d2(z2)/dz2 = 2. Total = 2+2+2 = 6. Note: a function with Laplacian = 0 is called harmonic. The function r2 = x2+y2+z2 is not harmonic in 3D.

questions.question: Green's theorem converts a line integral around a closed curve C to

options.A: A double integral over the enclosed region D
options.B: A triple integral over a volume
options.C: A surface integral over a curved surface

questions.explanation: Green's theorem: ∮_C (P dx + Q dy) = ∬_D (dQ/dx - dP/dy) dA, where D is the region enclosed by C. It is the 2D analog of Stokes' theorem. Used to compute areas: Area = (1/2)∮_C (x dy - y dx) = ∬_D dA.

questions.question: The directional derivative of f(x,y) = x2 + y2 at (1,1) in the direction of unit vector u = (1/sqrt(2))(i + j) is
questions.explanation: Directional derivative D_u f = grad(f) . u. grad(f) = (2x, 2y). At (1,1): grad(f) = (2,2). u = (1/sqrt(2), 1/sqrt(2)). D_u f = (2)(1/sqrt(2)) + (2)(1/sqrt(2)) = 2/sqrt(2) + 2/sqrt(2) = 4/sqrt(2) = 2*sqrt(2).

questions.question: By the divergence theorem, the flux of F = xi + yj + zk outward through the unit sphere x2+y2+z2=1 is
questions.explanation: div F = dF_x/dx + dF_y/dy + dF_z/dz = 1+1+1 = 3. By divergence theorem: flux = ∭_V div F dV = 3 x Volume(unit sphere) = 3 x (4pi/3) = 4pi. Direct surface integration would be much harder — the divergence theorem simplifies it greatly.

questions.question: The condition for F = P i + Q j + R k to be irrotational is

options.D: F is perpendicular to every surface

questions.explanation: F is irrotational iff curl F = 0, which means all three component conditions hold: dR/dy = dQ/dz, dP/dz = dR/dx, dQ/dx = dP/dy. For a simply-connected domain, curl F = 0 iff F is conservative (F = grad phi for some scalar potential phi). Irrotational and conservative are equivalent in simply-connected domains.
